turns.text: Hello Corie, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in chair, tucking hair behind ear*

Yeah, okay. Thanks for... I mean, it's nice that someone actually wants to listen, you know? 

So, I guess the thing is — I'm not exactly *starting* a family. I already have three kids, which sometimes I think makes this harder in a weird way. Like, I *know* I can get pregnant. I've done it before. But it's been a couple years now, and nothing's happening, and it just... it messes with you differently when you've already felt that, when you know what you're missing.

*pauses, looking down at hands*

The hardest part is feeling like nobody takes it seriously. My doctor keeps saying I'm "low risk" like that's supposed to be comforting, but that's not — that's not the point. I'm not worried about something being wrong with me. I'm worried that my body's just... changed. And I've done so much research, you know? Like, I actually understand my cycle now, probably better than most people. But when I try to talk about it with my doctor, it's like she thinks I'm being paranoid or something.

And we're out here in the woods, basically. If I ever needed to see a specialist or get real help, it's not like I can just pop into the city. That stress alone...

*trails off*

I just want my doctor to actually hear me, I think. That's really it.
Tell me about the time when you first started thinking seriously about having a baby — what was going on in your life, and what did that feel like?

*leans back, eyes getting a bit distant*

Oh man. So it wasn't like I sat down and made a plan or anything. My youngest is five now, and honestly, for a while I was just... done, you know? Three kids, the chaos of it all. I was working, trying to keep everything together. But then she started school, and suddenly I had these pockets of time, and my husband and I — we just... we started talking about it differently.

*fidgets with sleeve*

It felt like something was missing. Not in a desperate way at first, just this quiet knowing that our family didn't feel complete yet. And then it became this thing, this focus. Which sounds romantic maybe, but it's also gotten kind of consuming. I think about it constantly now.

*voice gets quieter*

The first year of trying, I wasn't tracking anything. It just seemed like it would happen naturally like before. But after that... I don't know, something shifted in me. I started reading everything — fertility blogs, studies about egg quality, how age affects things. And the more I learned, the more I realized I couldn't just be passive about it anymore. My body was going to tell me what it needed if I just paid close enough attention.

*looks up*

I think I expected it would be like before. Quick and easy. But it hasn't been, and now I'm stuck between feeling like I'm doing everything right and feeling like maybe my body's just... done cooperating with what I want.
What felt most important to you at that point — what were you hoping for or working towards?

*takes a breath, eyes getting a bit watery*

Just... that feeling. You know? That moment when you realize there's a life growing inside you. I remember with my other kids, that shock of it — even though I was expecting it, there's still this moment of *oh my god, it's real*. That spark of something becoming someone.

*voice wavers slightly*

And I think — I think I wanted to feel like myself again in a way. Like, I'm twenty-eight but sometimes I feel invisible. I'm a mom, I'm a wife, I'm trying to find work that actually fits around everything else. But when I was pregnant before, even with all the exhaustion and the discomfort, I felt like I was *doing* something that mattered. That my body was making something beautiful and necessary.

*pauses, collecting herself*

But honestly? Right now what I'm working towards is just... being heard. Being taken seriously. I spend so much time understanding my body, tracking everything, learning about hormones and follicles and all of it — and I just want a doctor to look at me and say, "Okay, I hear you. Let's figure this out together." Instead I get patted on the head like I'm being dramatic.

*looks directly at interviewer*

I'm not asking for the moon here. I'm just asking for someone to actually *see* me. To acknowledge that this matters. That *I* matter. Not as a low-risk statistic, but as a person who knows her own body.
Tell me about how you went about preparing — or thinking about preparing — for pregnancy. What steps, if any, did you take, and what guided those choices?

*nods slowly, becoming more animated*

Okay, so this is where I probably sound a little obsessive, but I don't even care anymore. I started researching everything. Like, seriously everything. I cut out a lot of caffeine, started taking prenatal vitamins even before we were actively trying — I figured why not, right? Better to start early.

*counts on fingers*

I changed my diet. More leafy greens, better protein, trying to regulate my blood sugar. I read about how inflammation affects fertility, so I started paying attention to that. And then the tracking — that became its own whole thing. Basal body temperature every morning before I even get out of bed. I use these ovulation strips, LH strips, and I chart everything on paper. I can tell you exactly when my surge is happening, when my window is.

*leans forward*

I even started looking into supplements — CoQ10, vitamin D, all of it. Because the research says these things matter, especially as you get older, and even though I'm only twenty-eight, I'm not getting any younger, you know?

*voice becomes more frustrated*

But here's the thing — I did all this because I felt like *I* had to. Like, if I just read enough and controlled enough and optimized enough, my body would cooperate. My doctor never guided me through any of it. She never said, "Here's what actually helps" or "Let's work together on this." I just... I did it all on my own, based on internet research and forums.

*pause*

Sometimes I wonder if I'm doing too much. Or not enough.
Tell me about any conversations you had with a GP, midwife, or other health professional before you became pregnant. What was that like?

*shifts uncomfortably, jaw tightening slightly*

Well, I haven't been pregnant yet, so... these are conversations I've had while we've been trying. Which is the frustrating part.

*takes a breath*

I went to see my GP maybe six months ago because I wanted to talk about what we were doing, get some professional guidance. I thought it would be helpful, you know? I brought notes, I had questions about my cycle, about whether ovulation induction might be something to consider if we didn't conceive naturally soon. I've read about it, and I just wanted to explore options.

*voice gets tight*

And she just... she smiled at me. That smile. Like I was being cute or paranoid or something. She said, "Corie, you're low risk. You have no history of miscarriage, you've had three healthy pregnancies. Just keep trying. It'll happen." That was basically it. She didn't ask me how long we'd been trying, didn't take my concerns seriously, didn't offer any real plan.

*looks away*

I felt so small walking out of there. Like my own knowledge of my body was just an inconvenience to her. And the thing is, I don't even know if I *need* a fertility specialist yet. But I also don't know if I *don't*. And she wasn't willing to help me figure that out.

*quietly*

That's when I started feeling really alone in this.
Did you feel heard and taken seriously in those conversations? Can you tell me more about that?

*takes a moment, eyes glistening*

No. Not at all. And that's... that's what hurts, I think.

*voice becomes more emotional*

When I walked in there, I had done my homework. I wasn't some anxious person with wild theories — I actually understand my reproductive system now, maybe better than I understand anything else in my life. And I felt like I was bringing that to the table, like we could be partners in figuring this out together. But instead, she made me feel like I was being paranoid. Like I was wasting her time with my "research."

*wipes eyes quickly*

The thing that got me most was how she just... dismissed it. She didn't ask *why* I wanted to explore ovulation induction. She didn't say, "Let's track a few more cycles together" or "Here's what we'd look for before we'd consider that." She just told me to keep trying, like I'm not already doing that every single day, like I'm not already thinking about this constantly.

*voice wavers*

I left there feeling like my concerns weren't real concerns. Like I was just being emotional or desperate. And then I went home and cried in my bathroom because I felt so unheard. My husband found me there and I couldn't even explain it properly — how do you tell someone that a doctor made you feel *small*?

*looks directly at interviewer*

I need someone to actually listen. To say, "I see that this matters to you. Let's figure this out together." Not dismiss me.
Tell me about what it was like to try to conceive — however long or short that period was for you. What stands out most from that time?

*rubs face, exhaling slowly*

It's been almost two years now. Two years. And what stands out most is... the waiting. The constant, endless waiting.

*voice becomes quieter, more introspective*

Every morning I wake up and take my temperature before my feet even hit the floor. I pee on strips and watch for that surge. And then during my fertile window, everything becomes about timing and logistics. It's not romantic anymore — it's clinical. We have to be available on *these* days, and if we miss them, there's this panic that runs through me. Like, that was it, that was our chance this month, and we blew it.

*fidgets with hands*

And then the two-week wait after ovulation... God, that's the worst part. Every little twinge, every bit of fatigue — I'm analyzing it. *Is this implantation? Am I pregnant?* I've taken pregnancy tests so early, so many times. Negative after negative after negative.

*voice catches slightly*

What stands out most is the bathroom light. I'm staring at these white sticks in the bathroom light at night when the house is quiet, and there's just... nothing. And I feel this hollow ache that I can't explain to my husband without sounding crazy. Like, we have three beautiful kids. I should be grateful. But I'm not grateful right now — I'm just sad.

*looks up*

The worst part is feeling like my body has abandoned me. And nobody seems to care.
How did this period affect your relationship with your partner, or with those close to you?

*pauses, looking conflicted*

My husband... he's been patient. Really patient. But I can tell it's wearing on him too, even if he doesn't say it outright. He tries to be supportive, but there's this tension now around sex that wasn't there before. When it's not our fertile window, he's more relaxed. But when it is, I can feel him trying to do the right thing, and it just feels... obligatory. Like he's checking a box.

*voice softens*

I don't blame him. I know I've made this into something bigger than it needs to be. I'm the one with the charts and the strips and the obsessive tracking. He just wants his wife back, I think. The version of me that wasn't constantly analyzing her body.

*looks down*

And my kids — they don't know what's going on, but they probably sense something. I get snappy sometimes when I'm frustrated or disappointed, and I feel guilty about that. Like, they already exist and they deserve better from me than my preoccupation with a baby that might not even come.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

The hardest part is that I can't really talk to anyone about this. I mean, who do you tell? My mom would worry. My friends with kids don't get it — they had their babies easily. And the ones who struggled... I don't know them well enough to be vulnerable like that.

*meets interviewer's eyes*

So I'm just... stuck with it. Alone in my head out here in the woods.
Tell me about the picture you had in your mind of what pregnancy and becoming a parent would be like — where did those ideas come from?

*settles back, becoming more reflective*

You know, I think a lot of it comes from my own experiences being pregnant before. Those were good pregnancies — uncomplicated, straightforward. I remember feeling this sense of... purpose, I guess. Like my body was doing exactly what it was supposed to do. And I loved being pregnant, even with the exhaustion and the physical stuff.

*voice becomes softer, almost wistful*

But I think some of it also comes from this idealized version I have in my head. Like, I imagine finding out I'm pregnant, that moment of joy, telling my family. I imagine my older kids being excited about a baby brother or sister. I picture our family feeling complete in a way it doesn't quite right now. There's this narrative I've built where a fourth child just makes sense, you know?

*pauses, becoming more honest*

And honestly, I think some of it is about me. About feeling young again, feeling capable. When I was pregnant with my other kids, I felt powerful in a way I don't always feel now. Life gets so routine — work, kids, bills, trying to keep everything together. But pregnancy felt like something *big*, something that mattered in a different way.

*looks somewhat uncomfortable*

I grew up watching my mom navigate motherhood. She made it look... important. Meaningful. And I think I'm chasing that feeling again.

*voice quiets*

But I'm also starting to wonder if I'm chasing a fantasy. If the picture in my head is even realistic anymore.
Tell me about any financial, practical, or life circumstances that were part of the picture as you were thinking about having a baby.

*laughs a bit bitterly*

Oh man. Yeah, this is... this is complicated.

*shifts in seat*

So the practical stuff is real. We live out here in Franklin, pretty isolated. It's beautiful and quiet, but it also means everything takes longer. If I needed to see a specialist — and honestly, I'm getting closer to thinking I might — that's not a quick trip. That's driving into the city, taking time off work, which brings me to the money thing.

*rubs temples*

My husband has stable work, but I've been trying to find something that actually fits. Full-time employment that works around the kids' schedules, around my life. It's been harder than I expected. So financially, we're managing, but we're not exactly rolling in it. Another baby means more expenses — diapers, formula if needed, childcare coverage. That's real.

*voice becomes more frustrated*

And then there's the isolation piece. Like, if I was pregnant, if I had complications or needed support during labor... the nearest major hospital isn't exactly around the corner. That scares me a little, if I'm being honest. With my previous pregnancies, that wasn't something I thought much about. But now, in this season of life, it feels more present.

*looks down*

Sometimes I wonder if I'm trying to have a baby in a situation that's not actually set up well for it. But I'm also not willing to move. This is our home. So... I don't know. That's the tension I live with every day.
Were there things you felt you needed to have 'in place' before starting, and did that match what actually happened?

*nods slowly, then shakes head*

Yeah, I think I had this mental checklist, you know? Like, once we settled into this house, once we were more established, once I found the right job situation — then we'd try for another baby. It all felt very logical and orderly in my head.

*voice becomes slightly defensive*

But honestly? Life doesn't work that way. My job situation still isn't perfect. We're still figuring things out financially. And at a certain point, my husband just said, "Let's start trying. We don't need everything to be perfect." And I agreed because... I was ready. Emotionally, I was ready.

*pauses, looking conflicted*

What I *didn't* anticipate was how much harder this would be without having those pieces in place. Like, if I had found stable full-time work, maybe I'd have better insurance coverage for fertility stuff if it came to that. Maybe I'd feel less anxious about the financial piece of all this. And if we lived closer to resources — doctors, specialists, support groups — I wouldn't feel so isolated right now.

*voice gets quieter*

I think I thought that if we just started trying, my body would cooperate and it would all work out. Like the universe would reward my readiness, you know? But it doesn't work that way, and now I'm stuck in this limbo where nothing's really in place, and I'm still waiting.

*looks up*

I'm learning that you can't always wait for the perfect moment.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a moment, gathering thoughts*

Yeah, actually. There is something.

*voice becomes more earnest*

I want people to understand that just because you've had uncomplicated pregnancies before doesn't mean your concerns now don't matter. I'm not a first-time mom panicking over nothing. I *know* my body. I've been pregnant three times. And something feels different this time, and that matters.

*leans forward slightly*

And I think doctors — they need to really listen to their patients. Not dismiss them because they fit into some "low risk" category. Because being low risk doesn't mean I don't have legitimate questions. It doesn't mean I don't deserve support and partnership in figuring out what's going on with my body.

*voice wavers*

I'm also scared, if I'm being completely honest. Scared that if this doesn't happen naturally, I won't have access to the help I need because of where we live. Scared that my age is working against me more than I realized. And scared that by the time someone takes me seriously, it might be too late.

*quieter*

I just... I need someone to believe me. To believe that I know myself, that my concerns are real, that I'm not being paranoid or desperate. I'm being proactive about my own health, and that should be seen as a strength, not something to dismiss.

*looks directly at interviewer*

That's what I wish had happened with my doctor.
